skill.name: meta-feasibility-analyzer
skill.description: Analyzes the feasibility of a proposed Meta-analysis topic by searching for existing Meta-analyses and Clinical Trials on PubMed/ClinicalTrials.gov. Use when you need to evaluate if a topic is viable for a new Meta-analysis.
skill.summary: Evaluates whether a proposed medical research topic is viable for a new Meta-analysis by searching PubMed for existing studies and clinical trials.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 52 static findings were evaluated. The 34 external_commands findings (backtick execution in SKILL.md) are false positives - they appear inside markdown code blocks as documentation examples, not executable code. The 3 weak crypto algorithm findings are also false positives identifying argparse and plain descriptive text as cryptography. The env_access findings for NCBI_EMAIL and NCBI_API_KEY are true positives but legitimate - the NCBI Entrez API requires these credentials for PubMed access. The critical heuristic finding about dangerous combination of code execution, network, and credential access is a false positive; the combination is expected and documented behavior for a PubMed API research tool. No prompt injection or obfuscation was detected. The skill is a legitimate medical research tool with transparent functionality.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access for NCBI Credentials
low_findings.description: The script reads NCBI_EMAIL and NCBI_API_KEY from environment variables for PubMed API authentication. This is legitimate and expected for medical literature search tools using the NCBI Entrez API, but users should be aware that credentials are accessed from the environment.

low_findings.title: External Command Documentation in SKILL.md
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains bash command examples inside markdown code blocks that instruct the AI to run Python scripts. These are documentation examples, not executable code, but they represent the intended execution model where the AI runs shell commands as part of the workflow.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Source URL in SKILL.md
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a hardcoded URL to the source GitHub repository. This is a legitimate source attribution, not a data exfiltration endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution via Python Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs the AI to execute Python scripts via shell commands (e.g., `python scripts/feasibility_ops.py search --query ...`). This is the intended design pattern for this packaged skill, but represents a capability for arbitrary script execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Credential Access for External API
dangerous_patterns.description: The script reads NCBI_EMAIL and NCBI_API_KEY from environment variables to authenticate with the NCBI Entrez API. The use of API credentials from the environment is a standard pattern, but documented here for transparency.

content.user_title: Analyze Meta-Analysis Feasibility with PubMed Data
content.value_statement: This skill helps you determine if a proposed medical research topic is viable for a new Meta-analysis. It searches PubMed for existing Meta-analyses and clinical trials, then evaluates whether sufficient new evidence exists to justify a new review.
actual_capabilities: Searches PubMed for existing Meta-analyses on a given medical research topic
Searches PubMed for relevant clinical trials and primary studies on the same topic
Extracts structured PubMed search queries from natural language topic descriptions
Evaluates feasibility based on presence of existing Meta-analyses and new clinical trial data
Provides detailed summaries of found literature with publication dates and titles
Determines research gaps by comparing dates of existing Meta-analyses against newer clinical trials
limitations: Requires NCBI_EMAIL environment variable to be configured for PubMed API access
Only searches PubMed database, not Embase, Web of Science, or other literature sources
Limited to first 10 search results per query, which may miss relevant studies
Does not perform full-text analysis or quality assessment of identified studies

use_cases.title: Research Planning for Doctoral Students
use_cases.description: A doctoral student planning a systematic review can check if their proposed topic already has sufficient Meta-analyses published, saving weeks of literature review effort.

use_cases.title: Grant Proposal Preparation
use_cases.description: A researcher preparing a grant application needs documented evidence that their proposed Meta-analysis fills a genuine gap in the published literature.

use_cases.title: Literature Review Triage
use_cases.description: A research team receives multiple proposed review topics and needs to quickly prioritize which ones are most viable for Meta-analysis based on available evidence.
use_cases.target_user: Research teams and systematic review groups

prompt_templates.title: Basic Feasibility Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the feasibility of conducting a new Meta-analysis on the topic: [insert your medical research topic here]. Search for existing Meta-analyses and clinical trials, then provide a conclusion on whether the topic is viable.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick check for a single research topic

prompt_templates.title: Customized PubMed Query
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to evaluate the viability of a Meta-analysis on [topic]. Use the following keywords for the PubMed search: [list keywords]. Search for both existing Meta-analyses and clinical trials, and compare publication dates to determine if new evidence exists.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have specific search terms or MeSH terms in mind

prompt_templates.title: Multiple Topic Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: I have three potential Meta-analysis topics: [Topic A], [Topic B], and [Topic C]. For each topic, search for existing Meta-analyses and clinical trials. Rank the topics by feasibility and explain your reasoning for each.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comparing feasibility across multiple candidate topics

prompt_templates.title: Detailed Gap Analysis with Timeframes
prompt_templates.prompt: For the topic [topic], perform a detailed feasibility analysis. Search PubMed for Meta-analyses published in the last 10 years. For each existing Meta-analysis, note its publication year. Then search for clinical trials. Determine if any clinical trials were published after the most recent Meta-analysis. Provide a detailed timeline and recommendation.

output_examples.input: Is the topic of bevacizumab for ovarian cancer viable for a new Meta-analysis?
output_examples.output: After searching PubMed, I found 3 existing Meta-analyses on bevacizumab for ovarian cancer, the most recent published in 2022. I also found 12 new clinical trials published after 2022. Since new clinical trials exist after the latest Meta-analysis, this topic is feasible for an updated Meta-analysis.

output_examples.input: Check feasibility of aspirin for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease
output_examples.output: Search identified 8 existing Meta-analyses on aspirin for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease, with the most recent published in 2024. No new clinical trials were found after that date. This topic is not feasible as it is already well-covered in the existing literature.

output_examples.input: Can I do a Meta-analysis on AI-assisted radiology diagnosis?
output_examples.output: PubMed search returned 2 existing Meta-analyses on AI-assisted radiology diagnosis from 2023 and 2024. Four clinical trials were found, with 2 published after the 2024 Meta-analysis. The topic is feasible for an updated Meta-analysis focusing on the newer studies.

best_practices: Configure the NCBI_EMAIL environment variable with a valid email address before using this skill to ensure reliable PubMed API access.
Use specific and well-defined medical topic titles for the most accurate feasibility assessment.
Review the dates of existing Meta-analyses carefully alongside new clinical trial dates to make an informed feasibility determination.
anti_patterns: Using vague or overly broad topics that return unmanageable numbers of search results and unclear feasibility conclusions.
Skipping manual verification of cited literature dates and accepting feasibility conclusions without reviewing the evidence.
Using this skill for non-medical research topics that are not indexed in the PubMed database.

faq.question: Do I need to install any dependencies to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill requires Python 3.10 or higher and the BioPython package for PubMed API access.

faq.question: What environment variables are required?
faq.answer: Set NCBI_EMAIL to your email address. Optionally set NCBI_API_KEY for higher API rate limits from the NCBI Entrez service.

faq.question: How many search results does the skill retrieve?
faq.answer: The skill retrieves up to 10 results per search for both clinical trials and Meta-analyses.

faq.question: What databases does this skill search?
faq.answer: The skill searches PubMed, which includes MEDLINE and PubMed Central. It does not search Embase, Web of Science, or Cochrane.

faq.question: Can this skill guarantee that no Meta-analysis exists on a topic?
faq.answer: No. The search depends on the quality of the query and PubMed indexing. Some relevant Meta-analyses may be missed if they use different terminology.

faq.question: Does the skill access any external services?
faq.answer: Yes, it accesses the NCBI Entrez API to search PubMed. This requires an active internet connection.
